message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Despite empirical evidence supporting vaccine effectiveness, vaccine hesitancy continues to thrive. Demand as a behavioral economic process provides useful indices for evaluating vaccine acceptance likelihood in individuals and groups. Using this framework, our study investigates the dynamics governing vaccine acceptance in two culturally dissimilar countries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Hypothetical purchase tasks (HPTs) assessed how Nigerian and US participants varied vaccine acceptance as a function of hospitalization risks due to vaccination (
                    <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                    = 109). Aggregate and individual demand indices (
                    <jats:italic>Q</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>max</jats:sub>
                    ) were computed with nonlinear regressions. Secondary analyses were conducted using repeated measures ANOVAs with vaccine type (COVID-19 and HIV) as the within-subject factor; country, age, and socioeconomic status as between-subjects factors; demand indices served as dependent variables.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Demand indices varied significantly as a function of vaccine type (
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                    (1, 57) = 17.609,
                    <jats:italic>p <</jats:italic>
                    .001,
                    <jats:inline-formula>
                      <jats:alternatives>
                        <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="23293101v1_inline1.gif"/>
                      </jats:alternatives>
                    </jats:inline-formula>
                    ). Demand for HIV vaccines was higher relative to COVID19 vaccines. Interactions between vaccine type and country of origin (
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                    (1, 56) = 4.001,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = .05,
                    <jats:inline-formula>
                      <jats:alternatives>
                        <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="23293101v1_inline2.gif"/>
                      </jats:alternatives>
                    </jats:inline-formula>
                    ) were also significant with demand for HIV vaccines among Nigerian respondents higher than that of COVID-19 vaccines. This was reversed for US participants. Interactions between vaccine type, country of origin and age were also significant (
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                    (2, 51) = 3.506,
                    <jats:italic>p <</jats:italic>
                    .05,
                    <jats:inline-formula>
                      <jats:alternatives>
                        <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="23293101v1_inline3.gif"/>
                      </jats:alternatives>
                    </jats:inline-formula>
                    ).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These findings provide evidence that vaccine type can influence demand. The relationship between demand and vaccine type also varies as a function of country of origin and age. Significance, limitations, and future directions are also discussed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: WEIRD or not: A Cross-Cultural Behavioral Economic Assessment of Demand for HIV and COVID-19 Vaccines
